title: Spec Builders Roundtable: Financing, Margins, and Building Strategy
summary: A peer roundtable of spec builders from across the US shared experiences and strategies around financing spec homes, working with investors, and managing project margins. Participants discussed the tradeoffs between pre-selling versus selling finished homes, how to find private investors, and the challenges of competing at different price points. The group also explored the operational differences between production-style building and custom-finish approaches.

teachings.concept: Investor Return Threshold for Spec Deals
teachings.explanation: When raising private capital for spec builds, investors expect a minimum annualized return of around 25%. Below 20% it becomes difficult to attract money because less risky investments exist. Offering 25-40% annualized return is the competitive range for real estate development deals.
teachings.quote: 25 is the number. Below that it's tough to find money.

teachings.concept: Partner Capital Structure with GC Fee and Commission
teachings.explanation: A common model for spec builders without their own capital is to bring in a silent equity partner who funds the deal, while the builder charges a GC fee (around 10% of build cost) and a real estate commission on the back end, then splits remaining profits 50/50. This allows the builder to operate with little to no money out of pocket while still getting paid for their work.
teachings.quote: I use partners on all my new builds. So I come in with no money out of pocket. Partner gets 50% of the profit. I get 50% of profit. I do charge a GC fee of 10% ish of the build price and I do get a real estate commission on the backend.

teachings.explanation: Selling a spec home after full completion is generally preferable because buyers struggle to visualize unfinished spaces and the emotional impact of a completed, staged home drives higher offers. Pre-selling reduces risk by securing a buyer early but dramatically increases management complexity and time investment. Days on market also accumulate negatively if listed during construction.
teachings.quote: You get one shot to hit the market. You want to hit the market at the right time of the year for your specific market, but finished, punch list done, staged, pre-inspected, ready to go.

teachings.explanation: Building a spec home from start to finish requires a fraction of the mental energy and decision-making compared to a pre-sold custom home, where every choice must involve the buyer, their family, and their advisors. Builders should price in or account for the extra time and effort when pre-selling.
teachings.quote: It took 4 million percent more effort on my part to get it to the finish line than a spec house. Every decision has to run by the husband, the wife, the kid, the aunt, the uncle, their friend is an architect.

teachings.concept: Target Price Point for Spec Builds
teachings.explanation: Building in the sub-$600K or even sub-$1M range in many markets means competing directly with production home builders like DR Horton who have massive scale advantages in cost and financing. Builders are better positioned above $1.5M where they can differentiate on quality and finishes and avoid commodity competition.
teachings.quote: I need to be in the 1.5 million plus because down at the 600,000 I'm competing with all of these other companies that build a track home and just slap them up super quick and I don't want to be competing with that anyway.

teachings.explanation: No single bank will reliably fund spec construction indefinitely, as banks periodically pause new construction lending. Builders should cultivate relationships with multiple local banks simultaneously so they always have access to capital when needed.
teachings.quote: There's three banks that I'm using right now because periodically the banks stop lending on new construction for spec. So you have to build relationships with several banks.

teachings.concept: Using Land as Collateral Instead of Equity Partners
teachings.explanation: If a builder already owns lots outright, using those lots as collateral for a bank construction loan is far more capital-efficient than bringing in an equity partner and giving up 50% of profits. The cost of bank debt at 7.5-8.5% is dramatically cheaper than the effective cost of equity.
teachings.quote: If you own the lots, why have you thought about just using them as the collateral for a bank loan? Because you'll save a lot of money. If you're talking about giving up 50% of your equity versus paying between seven and a half and eight and a half percent, those are drastically different returns.

teachings.concept: Finding Investors Through Soft Pitching and Social Marketing
teachings.explanation: The most effective way to find private investors is through genuine relationship-building, not formal solicitation. Sharing your work on social media and telling your network what you are doing attracts people who admire the work and want exposure to real estate without the operational burden. Keeping the ask low-pressure ensures only genuinely interested investors say yes.
teachings.quote: I want to make it really easy for them to say no because if you want to say no I don't want you. I don't want your money. I don't want you to ask me about everything.

actionable_advice: Build relationships with at least three local banks for construction lending so you have options when one stops funding spec projects.
Set a minimum profit margin target of 20% of total project costs (including interest, selling fees, and holding costs) before proceeding with a spec build.
When pre-selling a spec home, take a substantial non-refundable deposit (e.g., $200,000 or around 20%) to protect against buyer-driven changes and project abandonment.
Limit buyer customization on pre-sold specs to finishes only (fixtures, paint) and convert all selections to allowances so cost overruns are paid by the buyer at time of incurrence.
If you own lots free and clear, explore using them as collateral for a bank construction loan before approaching equity investors and giving up profit share.
Keep the number of investors per project as low as possible, ideally one, to minimize management overhead and communication burden.
List a completed spec home only after the punch list is done, the home is staged, and it has been pre-inspected so it is fully market-ready at launch.
If a home is listed during construction and accumulates days on market, take it off the MLS before completion, reset the clock, and relist when finished.
Work with agents who offer private exclusive or off-market listing options to continue marketing a home during construction without accumulating public days on market.
Pitch your track record and presentation materials to mortgage brokers as potential private investors, as they often want real estate investment exposure.
client_challenges: A first-time spec builder in Utah has a finished home sitting on the market for over two months with only lowball offers coming in, partly because it was listed during construction and accumulated days on market.
A new spec builder in Dallas owns three lots outright but is unsure how to find construction financing and investors to fund their first spec build.

A builder in Utah completed a spec home at the $1.1M price point in a market where buyers are interest-rate sensitive, and is now questioning whether they should have built at a higher price point where buyers are less affected by rates.
A boutique remodeler in Austin wants to break into the new construction market but is being passed over for new build projects because the market perceives them only as a remodeler.
A builder in St. George, Utah is constrained to one spec at a time due to self-funding, has limited bank options for spec construction loans, and is unsure how to scale.
Multiple participants raised the challenge of competing with buyers and builders who cut corners on flips and new builds, making it hard to compete while doing work correctly.
A participant building a 26-acre subdivision in Connecticut is navigating their first road installation and managing the complexity and financing of a much larger project than previous builds.
Participants questioned whether to build production-style with standardized plans and finishes or treat each spec as a unique custom home, and how that decision affects efficiency and margins.
notable_quotes: On flipping vs. new construction: 'The only way to make money in flips in Houston is to shut your eyes when you see a problem and just paper over it. And that's not something that I can do.'

On pre-selling complexity: 'It took 4 million percent more effort on my part to get it to the finish line than a spec house. Every decision has to run by the husband, the wife, the kid, the aunt, the uncle, their friend is an architect.'
On raising investor capital: 'I want to make it really easy for them to say no because if you want to say no I don't want you. I don't want your money. I don't want you to ask me about everything.'
On investor motivation: 'A lot of the people that invest in me are kind of living vicariously through me. They wish they were builders or developers.'

On market positioning: 'It'll give you a lot of credibility in the construction world and the real estate world. My average price point on the sales side just skyrocketed. Start doing spec houses and it'll open up a lot of doors for you.'
On investor minimum returns: 'Below 20%, like, because there's less risky investments. 25 is the number.'
